1:
"Then again Abraham took a wife, and her name was Keturah." The witnesses note Abraham's marriage to Keturah. On "Then again Abraham took a wife," the grammatical voices note the marriage. Ellicott and Gill note that "Then again Abraham took a wife, and her name was Keturah" — after Sarah's death (ch. 23) and Isaac's marriage (ch. 24), Abraham took another wife, Keturah; the patriarch, still vigorous (though the marriage may, by some, have begun earlier), married Keturah; the sons of this marriage (vv.2-4) founding further nations (the Arabian tribes); the continuance of Abraham's house.

On "her name was Keturah," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the wife. Calvin and Gill note that Keturah is later called a concubine (v.6; 1 Chronicles 1:32) — a secondary wife; the sons by her not sharing the inheritance with Isaac (vv.5-6); the marriage that brought forth the Keturahite/Arabian peoples; the wife distinguished from Sarah (the mother of the promised seed).

On the marriage, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the providence: Abraham's marriage to Keturah and the sons it brought forth show God's blessing on the patriarch (the promise that he should be a father of many nations, 17:4-5, further fulfilled); the founding of further peoples from Abraham; yet the covenant-line kept distinct in Isaac (vv.5-6). The verse displays Abraham's marriage to Keturah.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the marriage — "Then again Abraham took a wife, and her name was Keturah," Abraham marrying Keturah (Ellicott, Gill); Keturah a concubine/secondary wife (v.6; 1 Chronicles 1:32, Calvin, Gill); the founding of further nations from Abraham (17:4-5, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the marrying Abraham. The verse marks the wife: Keturah. The verse displays the marriage: again. The verse displays the sons: to come. The verse displays the nations: founded. The marriage displays Abraham's house continued. The verse displays the Abraham: marrying. The verse displays the Keturah: his wife. The verse displays the peoples: begun. Abraham's marriage to Keturah displays the further fruitfulness of the patriarch: Abraham again took a wife, Keturah — the secondary wife (v.6) whose sons would found further nations (17:4-5), the covenant-line yet kept distinct in Isaac. The verse displays the married Abraham, with Keturah's sons (vv.2-4).
2:
"And she bare him Zimran, and Jokshan, and Medan, and Midian, and Ishbak, and Shuah." The witnesses note the sons of Keturah. The grammatical voices note the names. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "she bare him Zimran, and Jokshan, and Medan, and Midian, and Ishbak, and Shuah" — Keturah bore Abraham six sons, the heads of various Arabian tribes/peoples (Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, Shuah); the founding of further nations from Abraham; the Keturahite peoples of Arabia.

On "Midian," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the notable son. Clarke and Ellicott note that among the sons, Midian (the father of the Midianites) is the most notable in the later history (the Midianites, Exodus 2:15; Numbers 22; Judges 6-8); the heads of peoples; the spreading of Abraham's seed into the Arabian tribes.

On the sons, the grammatical-historical note the nations: the six sons of Keturah headed various tribes of Arabia (the Keturahite Arabs); the fulfillment of the promise that Abraham should be a father of many nations (17:4-5); the dividing of the peoples from Abraham, the covenant-line yet in Isaac. The verse displays the sons of Keturah.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the names — "she bare him Zimran, and Jokshan... and Midian... and Shuah," the six sons of Keturah, heads of tribes (Keil, Gill); "Midian," the notable son (the Midianites, Clarke, Ellicott); the founding of Arabian peoples (17:4-5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Keturah's sons. The verse marks the sons: six. The verse displays the Midian: notable. The verse displays the tribes: founded. The verse displays the nations: Arabian. The list displays the sons of Keturah heading peoples. These Keturahite tribes, ranging the deserts east of Canaan, are reckoned among the children of the east (cf. Judges 6:3), and their descent from Abraham further displays the promise that he should be a father of many nations (Genesis 17:4), the covenant-line yet kept distinct in Isaac. The verse displays the Keturah: bearing. The verse displays the sons: named. The verse displays the peoples: begun. The sons of Keturah display the further nations from Abraham: Keturah bore Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah — the heads of various Arabian tribes (Midian the most notable), the fulfillment of the promise of many nations (17:4-5). The verse displays Keturah's sons, continued (vv.3-4).
3:
"And Jokshan begat Sheba, and Dedan. And the sons of Dedan were Asshurim, and Letushim, and Leummim." The witnesses note the descendants of Jokshan. The grammatical voices note the line. Ellicott and Gill note that "Jokshan begat Sheba, and Dedan. And the sons of Dedan were Asshurim, and Letushim, and Leummim" — the genealogy of Jokshan (Keturah's son): Sheba and Dedan, and Dedan's sons (Asshurim, Letushim, Leummim); the heads of further Arabian tribes; the spreading of the Keturahite line into peoples.

On "Sheba, and Dedan," the witnesses note the tribes. Gill notes that Sheba and Dedan were heads of Arabian peoples (the Sabeans and Dedanites, trading peoples of Arabia, mentioned later, Isaiah 21:13; Ezekiel 27:20); the tribes descended from Jokshan; the Keturahite Arabs.

On "Asshurim, and Letushim, and Leummim," the grammatical-historical note the peoples: the sons of Dedan (Asshurim, Letushim, Leummim) — heads of further tribes or clans of Arabia; the dividing of the line into peoples; the genealogy tracing the Keturahite nations from Abraham. The verse displays the descendants of Jokshan.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the line — "Jokshan begat Sheba, and Dedan. And the sons of Dedan...," the descendants of Jokshan (Ellicott, Gill); "Sheba, and Dedan," the Arabian tribes (Isaiah 21:13, Gill); "Asshurim, and Letushim, and Leummim," the further peoples (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Jokshan's line. The verse marks the sons: Sheba and Dedan. The verse displays the Dedan: his sons. The verse displays the tribes: Arabian. The verse displays the line: traced. The verse displays Jokshan's line, with Midian's sons (v.4).
4:
"And the sons of Midian; Ephah, and Epher, and Hanoch, and Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the children of Keturah." The witnesses note the sons of Midian and the close of Keturah's line. The grammatical voices note the line. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the sons of Midian; Ephah, and Epher, and Hanoch, and Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the children of Keturah" — the sons of Midian (Keturah's son): Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah; the heads of Midianite clans; the close of the genealogy of Keturah's children; the Keturahite peoples summed up.

On "the sons of Midian," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the Midianites. JFB and Gill note that the sons of Midian were heads of Midianite tribes (Ephah, mentioned Isaiah 60:6); the spreading of the Midianites (a people prominent in the later history, Numbers 22; Judges 6); the Keturahite Arabs.

On "All these were the children of Keturah," the grammatical-historical note the close: "All these were the children of Keturah" — the summing of Keturah's descendants (vv.2-4); the founding of the Keturahite/Arabian peoples from Abraham; the fulfillment of the promise of many nations (17:4-5); the line distinct from Isaac, the covenant-seed. The verse displays the sons of Midian and the close of Keturah's line.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the line — "the sons of Midian; Ephah... and Eldaah," the Midianite clans (Keil, Gill); "Ephah," the notable (Isaiah 60:6, JFB, Gill); "All these were the children of Keturah," the close of the line (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Midian's sons. The verse marks the sons: of Midian. The verse displays the Ephah: notable. The verse displays the Keturah: her children. The verse displays the line: closed. The genealogy displays the Keturahite peoples summed. The verse displays the Midian: his sons. The verse displays the Keturah: her line. The verse displays the nations: founded. The sons of Midian and the close of Keturah's line display the founding of the Arabian peoples: the sons of Midian were Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah; all these were the children of Keturah — the heads of Midianite clans (Isaiah 60:6), the Keturahite peoples summed, distinct from Isaac. The verse closes Keturah's line, before Abraham's bequest (v.5).
5:
"And Abraham gave all that he had unto Isaac." The very brief verse notes Abraham giving all to Isaac. The grammatical voices note the inheritance. Gill and Wesley note that "Abraham gave all that he had unto Isaac" — Abraham made Isaac his sole heir, giving him all his substance (and the covenant-inheritance); the son of promise (21:12) the inheritor of all; the distinguishing of Isaac from the sons of the concubines (v.6); the covenant-line and the inheritance settled on Isaac.

On "gave all that he had unto Isaac," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the heir. Wesley and Gill note that Isaac received "all" — the whole inheritance; the son of promise the heir, not the sons of Keturah or Hagar; the covenant-blessing and the substance settled on Isaac; the will of God (21:12, in Isaac shall thy seed be called) carried out.

On the inheritance, the grammatical-historical note the providence: Abraham's giving all to Isaac marks the narrowing of the covenant-line — Isaac alone the heir of the promise and the substance; the other sons provided for, but sent away (v.6); the line of promise kept in Isaac (and to be narrowed further to Jacob, vv.23, 33); the covenant carried forward. The verse displays Abraham giving all to Isaac.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the inheritance — "Abraham gave all that he had unto Isaac," Isaac the sole heir (21:12, Gill, Wesley); "gave all that he had unto Isaac," the whole inheritance to the son of promise (Wesley, Gill); the narrowing of the covenant-line to Isaac (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the heir Isaac. The verse marks the gift: all. The verse displays the Isaac: sole heir. The verse displays the promise: settled. The verse displays the line: narrowed. 6:
"But unto the sons of the concubines, which Abraham had, Abraham gave gifts, and sent them away from Isaac his son, while he yet lived, eastward, unto the east country." The witnesses note Abraham providing for and sending away the sons of the concubines. On "unto the sons of the concubines... Abraham gave gifts," the grammatical voices note the provision. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "unto the sons of the concubines, which Abraham had, Abraham gave gifts" — to the sons of Keturah (and Hagar, the concubines, v.12), Abraham gave gifts (portions, provision); the patriarch's fairness, providing for all his sons, yet not making them heirs with Isaac; the distinguishing of the heir of promise from the other sons.

On "sent them away from Isaac his son, while he yet lived, eastward," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the separation. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham "sent them away from Isaac... while he yet lived, eastward, unto the east country" — Abraham, in his lifetime, sent the other sons away to the east (Arabia), that Isaac might dwell undisputed in the land of promise; the prudent separation, avoiding strife over the inheritance; the keeping of the covenant-line and the land in Isaac; the other sons founding the eastern (Arabian) peoples.

On "eastward, unto the east country," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the dispersal: "eastward, unto the east country" — the sons of the concubines went east (to Arabia, the lands of the "children of the east," Judges 6:3); the founding of the eastern peoples from Abraham; the dividing of the nations; the covenant-line kept distinct in Isaac in the land of promise. The verse displays Abraham providing for and sending away the sons of the concubines.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the provision — "unto the sons of the concubines... Abraham gave gifts," the patriarch providing for all his sons (Keil, Gill); "sent them away from Isaac... eastward," the separation, keeping the land in Isaac (Calvin, Gill); "eastward, unto the east country," the founding of the eastern peoples (Judges 6:3, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the providing Abraham. The verse marks the gifts: to the concubines' sons. The verse displays the sending: away. The verse displays the east: country. The verse displays the providing Abraham, whose years are told (v.7).
7:
"And these are the days of the years of Abraham's life which he lived, an hundred threescore and fifteen years." The brief verse notes the years of Abraham's life. The grammatical voices note the age. Ellicott and Gill note that "these are the days of the years of Abraham's life which he lived, an hundred threescore and fifteen years" — Abraham lived a hundred and seventy-five years; the full age of the patriarch (a hundred years after his call at seventy-five, 12:4; seventy-five years after the promise); the long life of the father of the faithful, crowned with God's blessing (24:1); the close of his pilgrimage.

On "an hundred threescore and fifteen years," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the long life. Calvin and Gill note Abraham's hundred and seventy-five years — the long, blessed life of the patriarch; the fulfillment of the promise that he should die in a good old age (15:15); the pilgrim's days numbered; the life of faith brought to its full term.

On the years, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the pilgrimage: the days of Abraham's life (175 years) mark the close of his earthly pilgrimage (Hebrews 11:13); the stranger and pilgrim's days fulfilled; the patriarch, having seen Isaac married and the covenant secured, ready to be gathered (v.8); the long life of faith. The verse displays the years of Abraham's life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the age — "an hundred threescore and fifteen years," Abraham's hundred and seventy-five years (12:4, Ellicott, Gill); the long, blessed life (15:15, Calvin, Gill); the close of the pilgrimage (Hebrews 11:13, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the aged Abraham. The verse marks the years: 175. The verse displays the life: full. The verse displays the pilgrimage: closing. The verse displays the blessing: crowned. 8:
"Then Abraham gave up the ghost, and died in a good old age, an old man, and full of years; and was gathered to his people." The witnesses note Abraham's death and his being gathered to his people — the crux of the gathering. On "Abraham gave up the ghost, and died in a good old age," the grammatical voices note the peaceful death. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abraham gave up the ghost, and died in a good old age, an old man, and full of years" — the patriarch died peacefully in a good old age, full of years (satisfied with life); the fulfillment of God's word (15:15, thou shalt be buried in a good old age); the blessed, peaceful death of the father of the faithful; the close of his life.

On "gathered to his people," interpreters expound the phrase. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the meaning: "was gathered to his people" — a phrase distinct from death and burial (he was gathered to his people before he was buried, v.9, and his "people" — his fathers — were not buried in the same place); by the dominant reading, the phrase intimates the survival of the soul and its gathering to the company of the departed (the immortal spirit gathered to his fathers in another state); Clarke notes the phrase points beyond the body to the soul, gathered to his people in the world of spirits; a hint, even in patriarchal times, of immortality and the life beyond (cf. Hebrews 11:13-16, the better country). Some take it more simply as a euphemism for death (joining the dead, the ancestors), but the distinction from burial supports the fuller sense — the soul gathered to his kindred beyond.

On the gathering, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the hope: the "gathering to his people" expresses the hope of the patriarchs — that death was not the end, but a gathering to the company of the faithful departed; the immortality of the soul intimated; Abraham gathered to his people (the faithful dead), in expectation of the resurrection and the better country (Hebrews 11:13-16). The verse displays Abraham's death and gathering.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abraham died peacefully in a good old age and was gathered to his people (Keil, Clarke, Calvin, Gill). The phrase bears a crux: "gathered to his people" — distinct from death and burial (v.9), intimating (by the dominant reading) the survival of the soul and its gathering to the faithful departed (immortality, the better country, Hebrews 11:13-16) vs a simpler euphemism for joining the dead/the ancestors. The verse displays the hope: of immortality. The verse displays the gathered Abraham, buried by his sons (vv.9-10).
9:
"And his sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron the son of Zohar the Hittite, which is before Mamre;" The witnesses note Isaac and Ishmael burying Abraham at Machpelah. On "his sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him," the grammatical voices note the burial. Ellicott and Gill note that "his sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah" — Isaac and Ishmael, the two sons, together buried their father; the coming together of the brothers (Ishmael, cast out long before, ch. 21, here returning to honor his father); the burial of the patriarch in the tomb he had bought (ch. 23).

On "Isaac and Ishmael," the puritan and witnesses note the reconciliation. Gill notes the touching note — Isaac and Ishmael together at the burial; whatever the old estrangement (ch. 21), the brothers united in honoring their father; the reconciliation, or at least the coming together, at the grave; Ishmael acknowledging Isaac's precedence (named first).

On "in the cave of Machpelah... which is before Mamre," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the tomb: "in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron... which is before Mamre" — Abraham was buried in the cave he had purchased (ch. 23), where Sarah lay (23:19); the patriarchal tomb; the burial in the land of promise, in the secure possession (the pledge of the inheritance and the resurrection, Hebrews 11:13-16). The verse displays Isaac and Ishmael burying Abraham.

The thin comment matches the verse. The verse displays the buried Abraham, in the field he bought (v.10).
10:

The grammatical voices note the possession. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the field which Abraham purchased of the sons of Heth: there was Abraham buried, and Sarah his wife" — Abraham was buried in the field he had bought from the Hittites (ch. 23), where Sarah his wife already lay (23:19); the patriarchal tomb; the secure, purchased possession; the husband and wife reunited in the grave.

On "the field which Abraham purchased of the sons of Heth," the witnesses note the purchase. Gill notes that the field was Abraham's own (bought, ch. 23) — the secure possession; the first and only possession of the patriarch in the land of promise (a burying-place); the pledge of the future inheritance.

On "there was Abraham buried, and Sarah his wife," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the burial: "there was Abraham buried, and Sarah his wife" — the patriarch laid with Sarah in the tomb; the burial in the land of promise, an act of faith and hope (Hebrews 11:13); the husband and wife together in the grave, awaiting the resurrection; the covenant-couple in the patriarchal tomb. The verse displays Abraham buried with Sarah in the purchased field.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 11:
"And it came to pass after the death of Abraham, that God blessed his son Isaac; and Isaac dwelt by the well Lahairoi." The witnesses note God blessing Isaac after Abraham's death. On "after the death of Abraham, that God blessed his son Isaac," the grammatical voices note the blessing. Ellicott and Gill note that "after the death of Abraham, that God blessed his son Isaac" — after Abraham died, God blessed Isaac (the heir of the promise); the covenant-blessing passing to Isaac; God confirming to the son the blessing he had given the father (the promise continued, 26:3-4); the providence carrying the covenant forward.

On "God blessed his son Isaac," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the succession. Henry and Gill note that God blessed Isaac — the blessing of Abraham now resting on the son of promise; the continuity of the covenant; Isaac the heir of the blessing (and the substance, v.5); the providence over the covenant-line.

On "Isaac dwelt by the well Lahairoi," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the dwelling: "Isaac dwelt by the well Lahairoi" — Isaac dwelt by the well of "him that liveth and seeth me" (16:14; 24:62); the place associated with God's seeing and providence; the heir settled in the land of promise; the covenant-life continuing. The verse displays God blessing Isaac after Abraham's death.

The thin comment matches the verse. 12:
"Now these are the generations of Ishmael, Abraham's son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's handmaid, bare unto Abraham:" The witnesses note the generations of Ishmael introduced. The grammatical voices note the genealogy. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "these are the generations of Ishmael, Abraham's son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's handmaid, bare unto Abraham" — the genealogy of Ishmael (vv.12-18) is given before that of Isaac (v.19), as the line of the non-elect son is recorded and dismissed before the covenant-line is traced; Ishmael, the son of Hagar (the Egyptian handmaid, 16:1, 15), Abraham's son; the record of the blessing on Ishmael (twelve princes, 17:20; v.16).

On "Abraham's son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's handmaid, bare," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the parentage. Calvin and Gill note that Ishmael was Abraham's son by Hagar (the Egyptian, Sarah's handmaid, 16:3) — the son of the bondwoman (Galatians 4:22-23); not the heir of promise (21:10-12), yet blessed for Abraham's sake (17:20); the line of the flesh recorded.

On the generations, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the pattern: the genealogy of Ishmael (the non-elect line) is recorded first and briefly, then dismissed, that the narrative may proceed to Isaac (the covenant-line, v.19); the Scripture's pattern (as with Cain before Seth, Esau before Jacob, ch. 36); the blessing on Ishmael (the twelve princes, 17:20) noted, yet the covenant carried in Isaac. The verse displays the generations of Ishmael introduced.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the genealogy — "these are the generations of Ishmael, Abraham's son, whom Hagar... bare," Ishmael's line introduced (Keil, Gill); "whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's handmaid, bare," the son of the bondwoman (16:1; Galatians 4:22, Calvin, Gill); the non-elect line recorded before Isaac's (v.19, grammatical-historical). 13:
"And these are the names of the sons of Ishmael, by their names, according to their generations: the firstborn of Ishmael, Nebajoth; and Kedar, and Adbeel, and Mibsam," The witnesses note the first of the sons of Ishmael. The verse displays the birth and naming of Jacob.

27:
"And the boys grew: and Esau was a cunning hunter, a man of the field; and Jacob was a plain man, dwelling in tents." The witnesses note the contrast of Esau and Jacob. On "Esau was a cunning hunter, a man of the field," the grammatical voices note Esau. Ellicott and Gill note that "Esau was a cunning hunter, a man of the field" — Esau grew to be a skilled hunter, a man of the open field (the wild, roving life); the rough, active outdoorsman; the man of the chase and the field; the character that suited the world and the flesh (the profane man, v.34; Hebrews 12:16).

On "Jacob was a plain man, dwelling in tents," the puritan Henry and witnesses note Jacob. Henry and Calvin note that "Jacob was a plain man, dwelling in tents" — Jacob was a "plain" (Hebrew tam, perfect, upright, quiet, undefiled) man, dwelling in tents (the settled, domestic, contemplative life); "plain" meaning sincere, upright, simple (not crafty, but honest and quiet); the godly, settled man (a keeper of flocks, a dweller in tents, given to the quieter, more spiritual life); the contrast with Esau's roving worldliness.

On the contrast, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the two characters: the brothers differed — Esau the wild, worldly hunter; Jacob the plain (upright, quiet) man of the tents; the contrast of the carnal and the (comparatively) godly; the elder suited to the world, the younger to the covenant-life (yet Jacob's own faults would appear); the two natures, fitting the two destinies (v.23). The verse displays the contrast of Esau and Jacob.

28:
"And Isaac loved Esau, because he did eat of his venison: but Rebekah loved Jacob." The witnesses note the parental favoritism. On "because he did eat of his venison," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the partiality. Clarke and Ellicott note that Isaac's love for Esau was partly for the venison — a partiality swayed by appetite; the father's favoritism (a fault, the parents dividing in their affection); the love that would later be played upon (27:4); the weakness of Isaac.

On "but Rebekah loved Jacob," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note Rebekah's love: "but Rebekah loved Jacob" — Rebekah favored Jacob (perhaps mindful of the oracle, v.23, that the elder should serve the younger; and drawn to the quiet, plain son, v.27); the parental favoritism divided (Isaac for Esau, Rebekah for Jacob); the partiality that would breed the deception of ch. 27; the household divided in affection, with its sad fruits. The verse displays the parental favoritism.

29:
"And Jacob sod pottage: and Esau came from the field, and he was faint:" The witnesses note Esau coming faint from the field as Jacob cooked. The grammatical voices note the scene. Gill and Henry note that "Jacob sod pottage: and Esau came from the field, and he was faint" — Jacob was cooking (boiling) a pottage (of lentils, v.34); Esau came in from the field (from his hunting), weary and faint (hungry, exhausted); the occasion of the selling of the birthright (vv.30-34); the everyday scene that became the trial of Esau's heart.

On "Jacob sod pottage," the witnesses note the pottage. Gill notes that Jacob was boiling a pottage (a dish of red lentils, v.34) — the savory food; the occasion that Jacob would use to gain the birthright (v.31); the providence (and Jacob's seizing of the opportunity).

On "Esau came from the field, and he was faint," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the faintness: "Esau came from the field, and he was faint" — Esau, weary and famished from the hunt, came craving food; the faintness (hunger) that would lead him to despise his birthright for a meal (v.34); the carnal man, ruled by appetite; the occasion of his profanity. The verse displays Esau coming faint as Jacob cooked.

30:
"And Esau said to Jacob, Feed me, I pray thee, with that same red pottage; for I am faint: therefore was his name called Edom." The witnesses note Esau craving the red pottage, whence "Edom." On "Feed me, I pray thee, with that same red pottage; for I am faint," the grammatical voices note the craving. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Esau said to Jacob, Feed me... with that same red pottage; for I am faint" — Esau, famished, begged Jacob to feed him with the red pottage (the red lentil dish, v.34); the urgent craving of appetite; the carnal man, ruled by his hunger; the occasion of the bargain (v.31); "that same red" (literally, "that red, red").

On "that same red pottage," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the redness. Clarke and Gill note the "red" pottage — the red lentils (whence the name); the appetite fixed on the savory food; the craving that would cost Esau his birthright.

On "therefore was his name called Edom," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the name: "therefore was his name called Edom" — Esau was called Edom ("red") from the red pottage (and his own ruddiness, v.25); the name a perpetual memorial of his profane bargain (selling the birthright for a mess of red pottage); Edom the father of the Edomites (ch. 36); the name marking the man ruled by appetite. The verse displays Esau craving the red pottage.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the craving — "Feed me... with that same red pottage; for I am faint," Esau's urgent craving (Keil, Gill); "that same red pottage," the red lentils (Clarke, Gill); "therefore was his name called Edom," the name from the red pottage (Calvin, grammatical-historical). 31:
"And Jacob said, Sell me this day thy birthright." The witnesses note Jacob's demand for the birthright — the crux of what the birthright was. On "Sell me this day thy birthright," the grammatical voices note the demand. Gill and Wesley note that "Jacob said, Sell me this day thy birthright" — Jacob, seizing the opportunity of Esau's hunger, demanded that Esau sell him the birthright (in exchange for the pottage); the bargain proposed; Jacob's eager (if not blameless) seeking of the birthright (which the oracle had assigned to him, v.23, yet he sought by his own contrivance).

On "thy birthright," interpreters expound what the birthright was. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note its privileges: the birthright (bechorah) carried many privileges — (1) the dignity and precedence (the headship of the family, the rule over the brethren); (2) a double portion of the paternal inheritance (Deuteronomy 21:17); (3) by some, the exercise of the priesthood (before its establishment in Aaron, the firstborn acting as priest — though this is questioned); and (4), supremely in this covenant family, the spiritual blessing — the line of the covenant and the promise (the Messianic line, the blessing of Abraham, the inheritance of Canaan and the covenant); the birthright thus joined temporal dignity with the spiritual, covenant import.

On the birthright, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note its spiritual weight: in the family of Abraham the birthright was chiefly spiritual — the covenant-blessing, the promise, the line of the Messiah; to sell it was to despise the spiritual inheritance; Jacob valued it (though his means were faulty), Esau despised it (v.34); the birthright the token of the covenant-blessing. The verse displays Jacob's demand for the birthright.

The witnesses agree the substance: Jacob demanded that Esau sell him the birthright (Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley). The birthright bears its weight as a crux of what it was: the temporal privileges — the dignity/precedence, the double portion (Deuteronomy 21:17), and (by some) the priesthood — joined with the spiritual/covenant import — the blessing of Abraham, the promise, the line of the Messiah; in this family chiefly the spiritual inheritance, despised by Esau (v.34). The verse displays the birthright crux. The verse marks the demand: sell the birthright. The verse displays the debate: the temporal privileges and the spiritual. The verse displays the dignity: and double portion. The verse displays the covenant: the chief import. The demand displays Jacob seeking the birthright's blessing. The verse displays the bargaining Jacob, met by Esau's contempt (vv.32-34).
32:
"And Esau said, Behold, I am at the point to die: and what profit shall this birthright do to me?" The very brief verse notes Esau's contempt for the birthright. The grammatical voices note the words. Gill notes that "Esau said, Behold, I am at the point to die: and what profit shall this birthright do to me?" — Esau, ruled by his hunger, said he was about to die (of faintness, an exaggeration of his craving), and asked what profit the birthright would be to him (if he died); the carnal reasoning that valued a present meal above the future, spiritual inheritance; the contempt for the birthright.

On "I am at the point to die," the witnesses note the exaggeration. Gill notes that Esau, faint with hunger, claimed to be at the point of death — the exaggeration of appetite; the present craving overruling all; the carnal man's reasoning, that a dying man has no use for a birthright; the excuse of the flesh.

On "what profit shall this birthright do to me?" the grammatical-historical note the contempt: "what profit shall this birthright do to me?" — Esau despised the birthright, counting it of no profit (compared to the present meal); the carnal estimate, valuing the temporal above the spiritual and eternal; the contempt that marked him a profane person (v.34; Hebrews 12:16); the despising of the covenant-blessing. The verse displays Esau's contempt for the birthright.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the contempt — "I am at the point to die: and what profit shall this birthright do to me?" Esau valuing the meal above the birthright (Gill); "I am at the point to die," the exaggeration of appetite (Gill); "what profit shall this birthright do to me?" the contempt for the birthright (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the contemptuous Esau. The verse marks the claim: at the point to die. The verse displays the question: what profit. The verse displays the contempt: for the birthright. The verse displays the contemptuous Esau, who swears it away (v.33).
33:
"And Jacob said, Swear to me this day; and he sware unto him: and he sold his birthright unto Jacob." The witnesses note Esau selling the birthright by oath. On "Jacob said, Swear to me this day," the grammatical voices note the oath. Gill and Calvin note that "Jacob said, Swear to me this day; and he sware unto him" — Jacob, to make the bargain sure, demanded an oath; Esau swore (bound himself by oath) to sell the birthright; the contract sealed by oath; Jacob's care to secure the birthright (though his means were faulty); Esau's solemn, sworn surrender.

On "he sware unto him," the witnesses note the surrender. Gill notes that Esau swore — solemnly and irrevocably selling the birthright; the carnal man binding away his spiritual inheritance for a meal; the sworn bargain; the profane surrender of the covenant-blessing.

On "he sold his birthright unto Jacob," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the sale: "he sold his birthright unto Jacob" — Esau sold (gave away) his birthright to Jacob for the pottage; the despising of the birthright in deed (v.34); the covenant-blessing passing to Jacob (as the oracle had said, v.23, the elder serving the younger — yet here by Esau's own profane act); the birthright surrendered for a mess of pottage (Hebrews 12:16). The verse displays Esau selling the birthright by oath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the oath — "Swear to me this day; and he sware unto him," the bargain sealed by oath (Gill, Calvin); "he sware unto him," Esau's sworn surrender (Gill); "he sold his birthright unto Jacob," the birthright sold for the pottage (Hebrews 12:16, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the selling Esau. The verse marks the oath: sworn. The verse displays the birthright: sold. The verse displays the Jacob: secured it. The verse displays the surrender: profane. The verse displays the selling Esau, who eats and goes his way (v.34).
34:
"And Jacob gave Esau bread and pottage of lentiles; and he did eat and drink, and rose up, and went his way: thus Esau despised his birthright." The witnesses note Esau despising his birthright — the crux of the profane person. On "Jacob gave Esau bread and pottage of lentiles; and he did eat and drink, and rose up, and went his way," the grammatical voices note the meal. Ellicott and Gill note that "Jacob gave Esau bread and pottage of lentiles; and he did eat and drink, and rose up, and went his way" — Jacob gave Esau the bread and lentil pottage; Esau ate, drank, rose, and went his way — carelessly, as if nothing of moment had passed; the indifference of the carnal man, having satisfied his appetite and thought no more of the birthright; the casual, unconcerned departure.

On "thus Esau despised his birthright," interpreters expound the profanity. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note that "thus Esau despised his birthright" — the verdict of Scripture: Esau despised (held in contempt, made light of) his birthright; the profane estimate that valued a meal above the covenant-blessing and the spiritual inheritance; Paul/the Apostle calls Esau "a profane person, who for one morsel of meat sold his birthright" (Hebrews 12:16), and warns against the like profanity; the despising of spiritual and eternal things for the carnal and temporal; the mark of the profane (worldly, godless) man; Esau's contempt the proof of his unworthiness of the covenant (and the justification, in his own conduct, of the election of Jacob, Romans 9, and the rejection of Esau, Malachi 1:2-3).

On the despising, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the warning: Esau's despising his birthright is a warning to all — that the profane, valuing the present and carnal above the spiritual and eternal, cast away their part in the world to come for a momentary gratification; the carnal wishes that make men despise the heavenly inheritance; the sin and folly of Esau a perpetual warning (Hebrews 12:16-17). The verse displays Esau despising his birthright.

The witnesses agree the substance: Esau ate, went his way, and thus despised his birthright (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verdict bears its weight as the crux of the profane person: "Esau despised his birthright" — the profane (worldly, godless) estimate that valued a meal above the covenant-blessing and the eternal inheritance; the mark of the profane person (Hebrews 12:16), who sells the spiritual for the carnal; a warning against the like profanity, and (in Esau's own conduct) the proof of his unworthiness, justifying the election of Jacob (Romans 9).
